component_identification.content: Lords of Waterdeep base components include main board with action spaces and round track, player mats, agents (meeples) by color, score markers, adventurer cubes in four classes (fighter/rogue/cleric/wizard), coins, building tiles, quest cards, intrigue cards, and lord cards. Setup uses open quest display, intrigue decks, available building stack, and hidden lords for each player. The harbor area and owned-building spaces are central to turn flow and should be clearly laid out before teaching.

core_game_loop.content: The game lasts 8 rounds. In turn order, players assign one available agent to an open action space to gain resources, cards, quests, buildings, or special effects. Instead of assigning, a player may complete a quest by spending required adventurers/coins and taking rewards and VP. Once all agents are used, end-round cleanup returns agents, advances round marker, refreshes offers, and starts next round. Highest VP after round 8 wins.

detailed_rules.content: Action spaces are exclusive unless card text says otherwise. Board spaces provide adventurers, gold, quests, intrigue, or building opportunities. Purchasing a building adds a new action space to the board and grants owner benefits when opponents use it (as defined by building rules). Waterdeep Harbor has special timing: when you place there, you play an intrigue card and later retrieve/reassign harbor agents at round end in harbor order. Quests require exact resource payment and then grant immediate rewards (VP, resources, ongoing effects for Plot Quests, etc.). Mandatory Quests hinder opponents by forcing completion before other quests; timing these well can disrupt leader tempo. Each player’s hidden Lord grants end-game bonus points for specific quest/building categories, shaping long-term strategy. Intrigue cards can manipulate resources, turn order, VP, or quest pressure; always resolve full text. Common pitfalls: forgetting owner bonuses from built spaces, mis-sequencing harbor reassignment, and overlooking that quest completion can happen instead of agent placement on your turn. Teaching clarifications from rulebook-first play: always resolve effects in printed timing windows and finish mandatory steps before optional effects. If a card or tile text conflicts with a baseline rule, the specific card text takes priority for that interaction only. During teaching, call out hidden information boundaries, legal action checks before payment, and tie handling exactly as written. Most play disputes come from skipped sequence steps, so keep turn order visible and confirm each phase aloud in early rounds. Also enforce component-state clarity: players should separate spent versus available resources and keep any temporary modifiers visible until the step fully resolves, then clear them before the next player begins.

scoring_and_endgame.content: After round 8 concludes, final score is current VP track plus hidden Lord bonus and any end-game effects from completed quests/buildings/cards. Highest VP wins. Tie-breakers typically use remaining gold and then adventurers if needed by printed rules conventions. Because Lord bonuses are hidden until end, apparent board leaders can be overtaken during reveal. Final scoring should be performed category by category in open table order so everyone can verify each line item and avoid double-counting.

beginner_strategy.content: Identify your Lord’s scoring pattern early and draft quests that naturally align with it. Balance resource intake with completion tempo; hoarded cubes and coins do nothing without completed quests. Use Harbor when your intrigue hand offers meaningful leverage or combo timing. Buy buildings primarily when they improve your own action access first, with owner-trigger income as secondary upside. Save disruptive Mandatory Quests for moments when an opponent is about to spike a big scoring turn. Late game, prioritize immediate VP and realistic completions over speculative long engines. New-player coaching tip: choose one clear plan by the end of early game, then spend midgame turns increasing action efficiency rather than collecting disconnected benefits. Track remaining rounds/end triggers constantly; many losses come from mis-timing rather than bad tactical choices. When uncertain between two similar options, prefer the move that preserves flexibility for the next turn while still scoring now.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text as primary reference.
